sentences.text: We're now--
The family split.
The family split the affidavits.
My father was hidden up in the mountains.
And there is the infamous day arrives of Kristallnacht, November the 10th.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The building that we lived in, as I mentioned to you, was like a condominium.
One of the owners of the building, of his segment, was also the consul general of Guatemala.
And he had the Office of the Consulate within the property, and his mother also lived in one of the units.
And so always we had the emblem of the Consulate of Guatemala with, you know, the insignia of the state of-- and a flag was always flying on our building.
I saw it next to the one that were in our district where we lived.

And the reason they called it Kristallnacht is because when the windows broke, the stained windows and the beautiful windows, the-- the sound of the glass was like all the crystal broke all around, and it was totally devastating.
And that was the day they also tried to round up every Jew they could find and lay a hand on, rounding them up into trucks.
I saw the trucks because we lived on a main thoroughfare.
So a lot of things that had happened I was able to watch from the window, and you could see the trucks going by there filled with people.
And I said, Mama, what is happening?
And she said to me, you children get dressed in double clothing right now.
And we put on heavy clothing.
My mother had a feeling they were going to come and pick us up any minute.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] didn't take long, about an hour later, was a terrible banging on the door.
And there were two or three SS men standing there with guns drawn and said, where's the Jew?
Where are all the Jews?
And my mother said my father isn't here, and they came in to look.
They couldn't find my father.
And in the meantime, two other men rounded up all the people that lived in our building.
And before we knew it, they herded all the people into our apartment.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
And most of the people that lived in our building were Jewish.
I think there was only one or two families that weren't.
And somebody said, try to get the consulate, because-- the consul general-- because his mother was also pushed into our apartment.
In the meantime, they ransacked all the apartments in the-- in the building of the other people.
And they locked-- oh, we were there maybe 22 people, 24 people in our home.
And they locked the doors and they said, we'll get to you guys later.
We didn't know.
Are they going to burn us?
Are they going to annihilate us?
What are they going to do?
My mother said, you know, double clothing, triple clothing, get prepared.
We didn't know what they're going to do.
Took eight hours.
Finally, they found the consul general, and somehow he pulled strings.
And he says, you're on foreign territory.
You must leave this building.
Because he had to get his mother out.
And so I would say maybe the whole thing lasted for about 12, 14 hours until everybody was able to go back to-- in the meantime, people were hysterical because we didn't know.
Are they going to burn us?
And there was one lady that totally couldn't [? endure ?] and she kept saying, they're coming with the torches.
They're going to torch us.
They're going to burn us here.
We can't get out.
Because they barricaded the house.
You couldn't get out.
And we tried to console everybody.
And we tried to-- my mother was a very strong woman, and she was able to put-- instead of fear, she was able to talk to them and instill, you know, strength rather than fear into the people.
It was very traumatic.
And--
She instilled strength in us and she says, we'll stick together.
Nobody's going to separate us.
We'll be together, and I'll be there with you-- my sister, myself, and my mother.
As I said, she was a very strong woman.
And finally they released the people.
And of course, half of the stuff was gone of their homes because they ransacked the homes.
In the meantime, of course, my father's business, they ransacked the whole business.
And the big, you know, the Star of David was in front of the building.
And it really-- that was on the 10th of-- 10th of November that everything just went worse and worse and worse and worse.

And whatever, as I said, all his inventory, even though they gave us-- it was like a pittance and barely anything, you know, of when-- what was sold in the store, but at least there was something that he was able to get.
But on the 10th of November there was nothing left.
After that, it was-- the mob just walked in the-- I mean, they came in the front door, ripped all the clothing, everything, away, stripped the stores bare, and ran.
And nobody said anything.
I mean, you were just-- there was a Jewish star on the front of the store, and this is-- that's a-- a Jewish business, Jude.
Take whatever you want.
That's exactly what they did.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two or three days later, they put four Jewish families, just brought them over to our home and says, from now on, they live in your home, and they have no more homes here.
If you don't want them, they'll just have to go out on the street.
Well, of course we took them in.
We put mattresses, stuff, on the floor, you know, for people to sleep on.
And whatever we had, you know, we shared.
And that was really-- this is when hunger-- you know, you couldn't get as much to eat, and you had to share.
It was--
Oh, yes.
We learned what hunger is.
We learned it.
And also, sharing, I never had a problem with that.
If I had a piece of bread, let me share it with somebody.
And we've always-- there was never a question.
But it was getting worse and worse.
And we didn't know now, will we survive or won't we survive?
The existence became a very--
Extremely precarious.
And we were told-- we still-- oh, we-- we had a former maid that came in, says, we want your dining room set.
And I cleaned it for you, now you give it to us.
And things of that which we had to do.
There was nothing else to do.
And at that time is if you wanted to take any furnishings out, you had to pay to the government the retail value of your furnishings.
So if you had $100,000 worth of furnishings, you would have had to pay the government that amount.
And whatever you tried to ship out, they brought an inspector into the house.
Because we shipped some of the things because we couldn't take money with us.
I mean, at least so we had a few belongings that we-- we-- we bought our own belongings to ship them out and to send them the-- to America.

So that is what-- and there were inspectors that came to the house that watched every piece that went out and took inventory and put the amount down what-- what that piece would cost if you would buy it today.
And I remember my mother had a Bosendorfer piano, and she loved it.
Of course, we all played, you know, music.
So we shipped the Bosendorfer out, and we had to buy our own Bosendorfer.
So it was very strange during that period of time because money was really the-- was really the only surviving thing that you had.
And once we knew that we were all set with our affidavits and the visas for us, we were able to smuggle our father out on a banana boat to the British West Indies, which we did in December.
And we bought him passage on a banana boat.
We had no idea how he was going to survive there or whatever.
But once we knew he was on the high seas, we were able to go to the American Consulate and say, the visas, split them, he will have to take care of himself, and we are taking care of ourselves.
So we were able to-- by that time we had nothing left in the house anymore, because the few belongings we had, you know, we shipped to the United States.
The people were still living in our house.
We-- whatever we could, get food, you know, we-- we did.
We-- we bought it and fed everybody.
And then finally, in January we were able to leave.
And the fear constantly-- will we make it?
Will we get to Bremen?
That's where the boat left.
Will we make it?
Will the boat leave?
Will we be on the boat?
It was the constant feeling of survival.
Because many of our friends tried to get out.
They couldn't get visas to America.
They had to ship the children.
And many of my little girlfriends, they were shipped off to-- to England, and they became maids or whatever it was over there, and they never saw their parents again.
So in a way, we were blessed because my mother was with us.
And my mother also took care of her-- of her sister and her blind mother, and we took them with us to America.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, we stayed together.
And so we crossed the ocean in January, and we arrived in the United States on February the 2nd, 1939.
On a direct-- the boat went straight?
Direct.
We went on the big boat, the And I still remember.
It was 5:30 in the morning, 6 o'clock in the morning, we were entering the harbor.
And I knew all about the Statue of Liberty, and I couldn't wait to see it.
And we watched the Statue of Liberty, and our boat was pulling in.
And I remember we arrived, and we walked down the gangplank, and-- my mother and us-- she says, let's kiss the ground we're walking on.
And we kissed the ground when we arrived here.
We did kiss the ground.
And I have never, never taken this country for granted of what this country has given me and that this country made us survive, never.
I instilled that into my children, and they live by that too.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So now we are in the United States.
And there were some of our relatives had already arrived here, and we went to their house.
There was about 10 or 12 living there.
It was like Gorky's Everybody was on the floor with the mattresses.
But we were breathing.
How big a group were you now?
There was--

We had-- we brought my aunt, my grandmother that was blind, that we also had to pay additional funds so that she would-- she will never be part, you know, will never take from the government, and there was another uncle, three cousins.
I say we were about 12 or 14 people in a two-bedroom apartment-- excuse me-- stepping over each other, but being as happy as you can imagine.
Nobody had any money, but somehow we all pooled our few shekels that we had, and bread, at that time, was 10 cents a loaf.
And so became very entrepreneurial.
And I remember my mother had some jewelry that she smuggled out somehow, and we hocked it.
We went to the pawn shop.
And so at least we had a couple of dollars.
So then I started to knit.
That's where my handiwork came in.
And I knitted little sweaters that they paid me like $1.40 for a back of a big sweater.
And we started to be very entrepreneurial.
My sister went as a nursemaid.
And we lived in a little apartment in New York.
My mother was able to find a little apartment, and so we lived there.
And I-- I made believe I'm older, got a Social Security card, and I went to work in a sweatshop in New York on the sewing machine.
All I knew about an electric sewing machine was that if you step on it, it ran.
And so I did that.
I know I made the first day, I made $0.27.
Because the guy that-- that hired me says, girlie, you're on piecework, and there I was making little tobacco pouches.
It was so hot.
It was so terrible down there.
But you worked, and you held-- had your held-- head was high up.
And Papa was still in the British West Indies, so we relied on ourselves to do what we needed to do.
Yes, we were in contact, and somehow he was able to survive peddling.
He became a peddler in Trinidad.
And then we made a couple of dollars or he made a couple of dollars, and we sent him a bolt of goods, fabric.
And from one bolt of goods, it became six bolts of goods.
So he started to--
Trade, and pocketbooks and fabrics.
And after two and a half years, he already had become trading in copra, which was the-- the-- I think, oil or something like that.
And he was making a good living there for himself, and every penny he could he sent to us to help us.
And two and a half years, to the day, he got his visa to come to the United States.
And he arrived in the United States and started as a salesman, because he had studied English.
We all spoke English.
My mother spoke English.
We all did.
And by that time, I had already escalated to being a full-time operator, and I was making a few more dollars a week.
Everything was always piecework.
You worked piecework.
And that was the time when the unions started to become very strong, and I--
Yes, I was 15, but I told them I was 17, because otherwise I couldn't have gotten an Social Security card.
I had a very low number in Social Security.
So we-- the Social Security.
Yeah, I was 15 at the time when I was working.
And it was brutally hot in New York, and little, you know, young girls, they just passed out.
But you couldn't stop working, because if you stopped working you didn't make money, so you couldn't feed your family.
And my sister and I always said, Mama will not go to work.
We won't let her go to work.
We'll provide somehow.
So between her working as a nursemaid, I was working in the sweatshop.
And then at night I came home, and we took in homework, and we stuffed little animals, piecework, and we did that.
And I knitted on the weekends-- I'm-- I was knitting.
So we survived.
Somehow we survived.
We ate bread.
And my mother used to go, with gloves on, to the-- to the grocery store and she said, do you happen to have any fruit that may be a little bit tainted that I can buy cheaply?
And he says, lady, whatever you want, just go pick the fruit.
You don't have to pay me anything.
And she used to go there, very elegant, you know, nice, and bring home the fruit.
And all we did was cut off the-- the bad spot, and we made compotes out of it.

And then she went to the butcher and she said to him-- at that time we didn't have the supermarkets yet, you know, it was a butcher store and a grocery-- and do you happen to have any bones that I could feed my dog to?
Oh, he says, lady, I got all the bones with all the meat on it.
Just take it.
These-- this is what we ate.
And she cooked up the bones, and we thought it was so funny for my mother to be in a kitchen, because we had never seen her in a kitchen.
Oh, and one day somebody says, I have a chicken for you for $0.10.
I said, great, I'll pick it up.
And I took the subway for a nickel.
I picked up the chicken for $0.10.
I came home again and-- but there was only one problem.
The chicken wasn't plucked, and I had no idea how to pluck a chicken.
I didn't know that you had to put it in a-- in a pot of hot water.
So I sat all night long, and I was plucking a chicken.
So the next day we had a chicken to eat.
And cost me $0.10, but we had a chicken.
And we enjoyed that chicken.
We really enjoyed that chicken.
So these were just the wonderful things that we experienced and that make you of-- appreciative of every thing.
There isn't one thing in my life I take for granted.
I have learned that.
I've also learned I depend on nobody but myself, which is very important.
It's a very important thing you have to-- you have to learn to yourself.
Believe in yourself.
And I've always done that, and I've always pushed forward to that.
I believed in myself.
And I worked.
I worked hard.
There was no such a thing you work five days a week, 40 hours.
You work seven days a week, morning, noon, and night, and you make something of yourself.
And my sister did the same thing.
She started on the bottom with a company, bottom ground with a company.
She worked herself all the way up to the top in New York.
And then when my father came, and he became a-- you know, he started to, quote unquote, 'peddle.' He took things to sell them.
So he met some people, and one thing gave to the other.
And he started a little business, and he was manufacturing picture frames.
And by that time the war had broken out, so picture frames were a very good business because everybody wanted picture frames.
So when he came he said, I'm going into business.
And he said, Dorrit, you're going to work in my business, and you're going to run that business.
And I says, Papa, what do I do?
He says, I'll show you.
I was 16 by that time.
We had that business, and I ran that business.
We had 65 employees as we grew.
The business became good.
We grew.
And he expected-- he expected that of me, that I should be able to run a business, and I never questioned it.
So I cut my baby teeth, my father teaching me business, and he was so strict.
And he used to say to me, you don't have time for good times.
You just don't.
You just work.
That's all there is.
And I got my high school diploma two months after I came here, because with the education that I had in Europe I passed my-- immediately.
So basically, I didn't go to school in this country, with the exception of that short period of time.
And whatever I have learned, I have learned through life.
I don't have the formal education, that you would say that if you achieve a lot in your life that this is through total formal education.
I believe in learning, very, very important.
But it's actual hands on that will teach you what not to do rather than what to do.
And so we just kept going.
And unfortunately, my dad had a heart attack when-- after about four years, and as a young man, and lost his business.
But that didn't stop us from not going forward.
I did a complete turnaround, and I became-- I went into the garment industry.
And I became-- sales in a-- in the showrooms, and I was very successful in that.
And I also did modeling in the showrooms.
I was just about-- yes, you're right.
I was just going-- yeah, I was 20.
And I worked there full time.
And for that-- for-- for a 20-year-old or 21-year-old, the salary I received was very good.
It was $75 a week, which was incredible.
But I had the sales that I did there.
And I got quite an education in the garment industry, quite an education, which was good in many cases.
It-- it taught me of what not to do a lot of times of the things I saw and learned there.
But then I got-- I met a young man, and I got married.
An American.
An American.
And I worked.
I became pregnant.
I married in '48, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my son was born in 1950.
And it was not a happy marriage, but at that time you don't divorce.
You come from a Jewish house, you don't bring shame on the family.
I knew right away it was not-- something was not right, but I stuck with it.
And he was an interior decorator, and absolutely had no concept of money.
Spent money right and left, never worried about if we had to eat or we didn't have to eat at home.
In any event, I stayed with him, and I opened up a drapery and bedspread shop, and I had about six or seven employees.
And in 1959, which now was 11 years, I decided there has got to be something else in life.
And the only thing I did was I-- I took a suitcase, I packed the suitcase, and I took my little boy, who was 7 going on 8 at the time, I borrowed money for an airplane ticket.
My sister gave me $50.
I had an aunt living in-- in Los Angeles.
I went on a plane, and I landed in Los Angeles in 1959.
